figures.caption: Figure 1. Acute stressors affect six adult skin functions: physical barrier, pigmentation, defences, structure, neuroendocrine signaling, and thermoregulation.

tables.caption: Table 1. Summary of acute exposome stressors by affected skin function, biological mechanism, and clinical manifestation; the original table continues onto the next page for procedures and mask-related exposures.
tables.caption_ko: Table 1. 급성 exposome stressor를 affected skin function, biological mechanism, clinical manifestation으로 정리한 표. 원문 표는 procedures와 mask-related exposure 항목까지 다음 페이지로 이어진다.

cover_caption: Figure 1. Acute stressors affect six adult skin functions: physical barrier, pigmentation, defences, structure, neuroendocrine signaling, and thermoregulation.
source_title: Adult skin acute stress responses to short-term environmental and internal aggression from exposome factors

relations.note: Both reviews connect stress, damage responses, inflammation, tissue homeostasis, and aging-related biological decline, with this paper focusing specifically on acute skin exposome stress.

ai_summary.one_liner: A JEADV review that maps short-term exposome stressors onto six adult skin functions and their biological and clinical consequences.
ai_summary.problem: The chronic skin exposome and skin aging literature had been reviewed extensively, but short-term acute exposures were scattered across dermatology, immunology, neuroendocrinology, and cosmetic science.
ai_summary.solution: The review organizes acute stress responses by six skin functions and then synthesizes individual environmental, internal, procedural, and pandemic-related stressors.
ai_summary.key_result: The paper provides a practical stressor-to-function-to-manifestation map, with Table 1 linking solar radiation, pollution, weather, psychosocial stress, sleep deprivation, nutrition, hormonal variation, procedures, and mask use to mechanisms and clinical signs.
limitations: The review is narrative rather than a systematic review or meta-analysis.
Evidence types vary widely across stressors, from human exposure studies to mouse models and ex vivo skin experiments.
Several acute manifestations overlap with delayed flares or chronic exposure backgrounds, making causal attribution difficult.

research_gaps: Separate the effects of simultaneous acute stressors such as UV, pollution, humidity, sleep deprivation, and psychosocial stress.
Define when protective acute stress responses transition into chronic inflammation, skin aging, or disease flare.
Standardize acute skin-response biomarkers across TEWL, erythema, sebum, cytokines, ROS, microbiome state, neuropeptides, and cortisol.
Model individual differences from phototype, genome, chronic exposome background, skin disease history, and microbiome state.
future_directions: Design controlled multi-stressor human studies with shared barrier, immune, pigmentary, neuroendocrine, and microbiome endpoints.
Build acute-to-chronic exposome models that connect transient homeostatic responses to inflammaging and visible skin aging.
Test prevention strategies such as photoprotection, sleep improvement, stress management, moisturizers, antioxidants, anti-pollution products, and probiotics against acute-response biomarkers.
Use longitudinal wearable and environmental exposure data to connect real-world exposome bursts to skin symptoms.
